# Typology construction

## Purpose of the typology

The typology construction was used to group grid cells with similar green-space configurations. Instead of looking only at individual metrics, the typology approach combines several green-space metrics into broader spatial categories. This makes it easier to compare recurring green-space patterns across Delft and Xi’an.

K-means clustering was used to create four typologies for each city. The clustering was based on the following variables: green percentage, mean patch area, radius of gyration, contiguity, and nearest-neighbour distance. These variables describe both the amount of green space and its spatial configuration. Before clustering, the variables were standardised so that metrics with larger numerical ranges did not dominate the result.

The four typologies were interpreted using the mean values of the green-space and flood-related variables. The labels are not created automatically by the K-means algorithm. They were assigned afterwards based on the characteristics of each group.

## Delft typologies

The Delft typology map shows four distinct green-space configurations. Typology 2 represents the greenest and most contiguous cells. These cells have the highest mean green percentage, the largest mean patch area, and the highest contiguity. Typology 3 represents cells with almost no green space. Typology 4 represents low-green cells with small and more dispersed green patches. Typology 1 represents moderately green connected areas.

<!-- FIGURE:
Put the Delft typology map here.
Suggested file:
../figures/results/delft_typology.png
-->

![Green-space typologies in Delft.](../figures/results/delft_typology.png){#fig-delft-typology width=100%}

| Delft typology | Suggested interpretation          | Number of cells | Green % | Mean patch area | Contiguity |  ENN | FWEI change | Flood share |
| -------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------: | ------: | --------------: | ---------: | ---: | ----------: | ----------: |
| Typology 1     | Moderately green connected areas  |            2408 |    47.2 |           0.175 |      0.510 | 14.6 |       0.018 |       0.308 |
| Typology 2     | Highly green and contiguous areas |             690 |    79.3 |           0.760 |      0.855 | 11.0 |       0.031 |       0.462 |
| Typology 3     | Sparse green fragmented areas     |            2242 |     0.3 |           0.006 |      0.101 | 11.8 |      -0.007 |       0.225 |
| Typology 4     | Low green dispersed areas         |            4660 |    14.2 |           0.024 |      0.212 | 19.8 |      -0.001 |       0.131 |

In Delft, Typology 2 has the highest mean FWEI change and the highest flood share. This means that the greenest and most contiguous cells also show the strongest FWEI-derived surface water increase. Typology 1 also has relatively high flood-related values, while Typology 4 has the lowest flood share. Typology 3 has almost no green space but still shows a flood share of 0.225, which suggests that surface water increase is not only associated with green areas.

## Xi’an typologies

The Xi’an typology map also shows four green-space configurations. The same general pattern appears: Typology 2 is the highly green and contiguous group, Typology 3 is the sparse green group, Typology 4 is a low-green dispersed group, and Typology 1 is a moderately green connected group.

<!-- FIGURE:
Put the Xi’an typology map here.
Suggested file:
../figures/results/xian_typology.png
-->

![Green-space typologies in Xi’an.](../figures/results/xian_typology.png){#fig-xian-typology width=100%}

| Xi’an typology | Suggested interpretation          | Number of cells | Green % | Mean patch area | Contiguity |  ENN | FWEI change | Flood share |
| -------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------: | ------: | --------------: | ---------: | ---: | ----------: | ----------: |
| Typology 1     | Moderately green connected areas  |            3971 |    33.2 |           0.169 |      0.654 | 16.4 |       0.023 |       0.180 |
| Typology 2     | Highly green and contiguous areas |            1171 |    76.3 |           0.736 |      0.877 | 12.8 |       0.031 |       0.314 |
| Typology 3     | Sparse green fragmented areas     |            1702 |     0.5 |           0.012 |      0.253 | 14.4 |       0.018 |       0.089 |
| Typology 4     | Low green dispersed areas         |            3156 |    17.8 |           0.058 |      0.451 | 29.5 |       0.022 |       0.147 |

In Xi’an, Typology 2 also has the highest mean FWEI change and the highest flood share. This is similar to Delft. Typology 3, which has almost no green space, has the lowest flood share. Typology 4 has low green coverage and a relatively high nearest-neighbour distance, meaning that green patches are more dispersed and isolated.

## Comparison of typologies between Delft and Xi’an

The typology results show that similar green-space configurations can be identified in both cities, despite the differences between Delft and Xi’an. In both cases, the clustering produced a highly green and contiguous typology, a moderately green typology, a low-green dispersed typology, and a sparse green typology.

This is an important result because it suggests that the grid-based landscape metric method can identify comparable spatial patterns across different urban contexts. The actual locations of the typologies differ between the cities, but the general types are similar.

The strongest flood-related values occur in Typology 2 in both Delft and Xi’an. This typology has the highest green coverage, largest patch area, and highest contiguity. This finding should be interpreted carefully. It does not mean that highly green and contiguous areas increase flooding. Instead, it suggests that these areas are spatially associated with higher FWEI-derived surface water increase. This could be because parks, open green areas, and low-lying spaces are more likely to store visible surface water after rainfall.

## Typology interpretation

The typology construction helps move the analysis beyond individual correlations. While the correlation analysis shows the strength and direction of relationships between variables, the typology analysis shows how combinations of green-space characteristics relate to flood-related indicators.

The results suggest that the most flood-related surface water change is not found in the most fragmented green areas, but in the highly green and contiguous areas. This pattern appears in both cities. The lowest flood share is found in low-green or sparse-green typologies, especially in Xi’an. This may reflect the ability of remote sensing to detect water more clearly in open green areas than in dense built-up areas.

Overall, the typology results show that green-space configuration and FWEI-based surface water change are related, but not in a simple protective direction. The relationship depends on the spatial context of the green areas and the way surface water is detected.
